skill.name: citation-formatter
skill.description: Formats academic citations and bibliographies across 1000+ styles including APA, MLA, Vancouver, Chicago, and AMA. Supports batch conversion, reference validation, and auto-detection of input formats.
skill.summary: Format and convert academic citations between styles for journal submission
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 61 potential issues across 6 files, all of which are false positives. The 47 backtick detections are Markdown code fence syntax (```) used for formatting code examples, not shell execution. The 6 weak-crypto hits matched innocuous text strings in YAML frontmatter, argparse constructors, and DOI citation examples. The 4 system-reconnaissance hits were on documentation text. Hardcoded URLs are source attribution links and sample DOI data. The single file-write detection is the intended batch output function of the citation formatter. The Python script contains no network calls, no obfuscation, no dynamic code execution, and no data exfiltration. This is a legitimate academic tool with well-scoped functionality.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misidentified as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 47 instances of backtick characters (```) across SKILL.md, references/ama-guidelines.md, and references/examples.md as shell command execution. These are standard Markdown code fence markers used to format code examples and citation templates. No actual shell execution occurs from markdown formatting syntax.

low_findings.title: Documentation text matched weak cryptography heuristics
low_findings.description: Six text strings in documentation and code matched pattern-matching rules for weak cryptographic algorithms. The matches were on YAML frontmatter description text, argparse.ArgumentParser constructor, DOI number strings in citation examples, and hashlib-similar hex strings in the audit result file. No cryptographic code exists anywhere in this skill.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs flagged as hardcoded network endpoints
low_findings.description: Three URLs were flagged as hardcoded endpoints. The GitHub URL in SKILL.md line 7 is a source attribution link. The DOI URL in references/examples.md line 6 is sample citation data. The Python script makes zero runtime network requests — it uses only standard library modules (re, sys, json, argparse, datetime, typing) with no urllib, requests, http, or socket imports.

low_findings.title: Documentation text matched system reconnaissance heuristics
low_findings.description: Four text strings matched system reconnaissance patterns. These were all on innocuous documentation text: output discipline guidelines (SKILL.md:52), validation command instructions (SKILL.md:64), example print statements showing validation output (SKILL.md:126), and evaluation notes in the audit result file. No actual system probing or reconnaissance code exists.

dangerous_patterns.title: Backtick characters in Markdown code blocks (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer detected 47 backtick patterns across SKILL.md and reference markdown files and classified them as shell backtick execution. All instances are Markdown code fence syntax (triple backticks used to denote code blocks). This is a known false positive pattern when scanning markdown-heavy repositories with shell-focused pattern matchers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Batch file write operation in citation formatter script
dangerous_patterns.description: The batch_format function at scripts/main.py:527 opens a file for writing using Python's built-in open() with encoding='utf-8'. This is the intended output mechanism for saving formatted citations to user-specified files. The output path is controlled by the caller via command-line arguments, not hardcoded. This is legitimate functionality for a citation formatting tool.

content.value_statement: Manually formatting citations for journal submission is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill automates citation conversion between APA, MLA, Vancouver, Chicago, and AMA formats, ensuring reference accuracy and compliance with journal requirements.
actual_capabilities: Convert citations between APA, MLA, Vancouver, Chicago, BibTeX, and AMA formats
Auto-detect input citation format from unstructured or mixed text
Batch process multiple citations from input files for large reference collections
Validate reference completeness including required fields like authors, year, volume, and DOI
Generate in-text citations formatted for specific academic styles
Format references to meet journal-specific submission requirements
limitations: Requires Python 3.10 or later to run the packaged conversion script
Free-text citation parsing may produce incomplete results for heavily abbreviated or unusual formats
Journal name abbreviation lookup is limited to the built-in mapping of common medical journals
Does not resolve DOIs or connect to online reference databases for metadata lookup

use_cases.title: Journal Manuscript Submission
use_cases.description: Convert a mixed collection of references into the single target citation style required by a specific journal before manuscript submission.

use_cases.title: Grant Proposal Preparation
use_cases.description: Format and validate reference sections for grant applications, checking that all required fields such as authors, year, and DOI are present and correctly structured.

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Bibliography
use_cases.description: Batch process large reference collections exported from multiple databases into a consistent citation format for literature reviews and meta-analyses.
use_cases.target_user: Medical librarians and systematic review teams

prompt_templates.title: Basic Single Citation Formatting
prompt_templates.prompt: Format this citation into AMA style: Smith, J. A., Jones, M. B., & Brown, K. L. (2020). Effects of treatment on patient outcomes in clinical trials. Journal of the American Medical Association, 324(15), 1523-1531. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2020.12345
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a single reference in one format and need it converted to AMA style for a medical journal submission.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Between Citation Styles
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these three APA references to Vancouver style: 1. Zhang, L., Chen, Y., & Wang, H. (2021). Novel approaches to cancer immunotherapy. Nature Medicine, 27(3), 412-425. 2. Roberts, K. M. (2019). Statistical methods for clinical trials. Springer. 3. Lee, S., & Park, J. (2022). Machine learning in radiology. Radiology, 302(1), 45-53.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to switch a reference list from one citation style to another for a different journal's submission guidelines.

prompt_templates.title: Batch File Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a file called references.txt with 50 citations in mixed formats (APA, MLA, and Vancouver). Convert all of them to AMA style and save the results to formatted_ama.txt. Show me any citations that could not be parsed correctly.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a large file containing many citations in different formats and need them all converted at once with error reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Reference Validation and Completeness Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this reference list for completeness. For each reference, verify that it has: authors, title, journal name, publication year, volume, issue, and page numbers. List any missing fields for each reference. Here are the references: [paste your reference list here]
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to audit a bibliography before submission to ensure every reference has all required fields and is ready for journal review.

output_examples.input: APA format: Smith, J. A., Jones, M. B., & Brown, K. L. (2020). Effects of treatment on patient outcomes. JAMA, 324(15), 1523-1531.
output_examples.output: Smith JA, Jones MB, Brown KL. Effects of treatment on patient outcomes. JAMA. 2020;324(15):1523-1531.

output_examples.input: BibTeX: @article{zhang2021, author = {Zhang, Li and Chen, Yu and Wang, Hui}, title = {Novel approaches to cancer immunotherapy}, journal = {Nature Medicine}, year = {2021}, volume = {27}, number = {3}, pages = {412--425}}
output_examples.output: Zhang L, Chen Y, Wang H. Novel approaches to cancer immunotherapy. Nat Med. 2021;27(3):412-425.

output_examples.input: Free text: Smith et al wrote about treatment effects in the Journal of the American Medical Association 2020, volume 324 issue 15, pages 1523-1531
output_examples.output: Smith JA et al. Effects of treatment on patient outcomes. JAMA. 2020;324(15):1523-1531.

best_practices: Validate DOIs and URLs in formatted references before submitting to a journal
Check journal-specific formatting requirements beyond the standard citation style guide
Keep the original reference file as a backup before running batch conversion
anti_patterns: Do not trust auto-detected citation formats without spot-checking the output against the original source
Do not batch process hundreds of references without first testing a small sample to confirm correct formatting
Do not submit formatted references without manually reviewing special cases such as websites, preprints, and conference abstracts

faq.question: What citation styles does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports APA, MLA, Vancouver, Chicago, BibTeX, and AMA formats. The script can auto-detect the input format and convert between any supported styles.

faq.question: Can it process BibTeX files?
faq.answer: Yes. The script includes a BibTeX parser that extracts author, title, journal, year, volume, issue, pages, and DOI fields and converts them to the target output format.

faq.question: Does this skill require an internet connection?
faq.answer: No. All formatting and conversion runs entirely on your local machine using only Python standard library modules. It does not connect to external APIs or databases.

faq.question: How accurate is the free-text citation parser?
faq.answer: The parser handles common unstructured formats reliably, but heavily abbreviated citations or those with unusual formatting may produce incomplete results that need manual correction.

faq.question: Can I batch process hundreds of references at once?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the batch processing mode with an input file that has one citation per line. The script will process all citations and write the formatted output to your specified file.

faq.question: What Python version is required to run the script?
faq.answer: Python 3.10 or later. The script uses only standard library modules with no external package dependencies, so no pip install is needed.
